questionText: Solve the equation $\arcsin(2x - 1) = \dfrac{\pi}{6}$, giving the exact value of $x$ and checking it lies in the valid domain.

steps.explanation: The unknown $x$ sits inside an inverse sine. The strategy is to undo $\arcsin$ by applying $\sin$ to both sides, then solve the resulting linear equation, but first we check the equation can have a solution.
The inverse sine only accepts inputs between $-1$ and $1$, so the expression $2x-1$ must lie in that range for the left-hand side to be defined.
Adding $1$ throughout and dividing by $2$ shows any valid solution must lie in $[0,1]$; we will confirm our answer against this.

steps.description: Range check on the right-hand side
steps.explanation: The output of $\arcsin$ always lies in $\left[-\tfrac\pi2,\tfrac\pi2\right]$; since $\tfrac\pi6$ is inside this range, a solution is possible.
Applying $\sin$ is the inverse operation to $\arcsin$, so it will free the unknown from inside the inverse function.
Because $2x-1\in[-1,1]$, the composition $\sin(\arcsin u)$ returns $u$ exactly, leaving $2x-1$.
The angle $\tfrac\pi6$ is $30^\circ$, one of the standard angles whose sine is known exactly from half of an equilateral triangle.

steps.description: Evaluate sin of pi over six
steps.explanation: From the $30$-$60$-$90$ triangle, $\sin 30^\circ=\tfrac12$, giving the exact right-hand side.
Equating the two simplified sides leaves a straightforward linear equation in $x$.
Adding $1$ (which is $\tfrac22$) to $\tfrac12$ gives $\tfrac32$, isolating the $2x$ term.
Dividing both sides by $2$ gives the candidate solution.
The value lies in the allowed interval from Step 3, so it is a genuine solution rather than an extraneous one.
Feeding $x=\tfrac34$ back in gives $\arcsin\tfrac12$, which is indeed $\tfrac\pi6$, confirming the solution.

steps.workingLatex: \arcsin \text{ is one-to-one} \ \Rightarrow\ x = \tfrac{3}{4} \text{ is the only solution}
steps.explanation: Because $\arcsin$ is a one-to-one function, applying $\sin$ did not introduce extra solutions, so $x=\tfrac34$ is unique.
This is the exact and only solution of the equation.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $x=\tfrac34$, which lies in the required interval $[0,1]$ and satisfies $\arcsin\tfrac12=\tfrac\pi6$.
